answer.rationale: The simpliciter doctrine that Christ died in the place of sinners is canonically demonstrated by the convergence of Isa 53:4-6 (paga H6293 hiphil, YHWH causes the iniquity of us all to fall on the servant), Mark 10:45 with Matt 20:28 (lytron G3083 anti G473 pollon, a ransom in-place-of many on Jesus's own lips), and the dense Pauline-Petrine hyper G5228 network (Rom 5:6-8, 2 Cor 5:14-21, Gal 1:4, Gal 3:13, 1 Pet 2:24, 1 Pet 3:18, Heb 9:28); the prepositions anti and hyper, the noun lytron, and the hilasmos G2434 / hilasterion G2435 sacrificial vocabulary together carry the substitution verdict at the lexical level.
answer.notes: This question targets substitutionary atonement simpliciter (Christ died in the place of sinners), not the specific Reformed penal-satisfaction theory of how that substitution operates. The simpliciter doctrine is universally affirmed across every historic Christian lineage: patristic Christus-Victor (Athanasius, Gregory of Nyssa), Anselmian satisfaction, Catholic-magisterial (CCC 599-618), Lutheran-sacramental (Augsburg III), Anglican (Article 2), Reformed (WCF 8, Heidelberg Q37-40), Methodist (Articles II, XX), Anabaptist (Dordrecht), Pentecostal (AG Statement of Fundamental Truths), Eastern Orthodox (Christ took the curse of death and triumphed over it in our place). Lineages diverge on the MECHANISM (penal payment vs satisfaction of honor vs triumph over death and devil vs recapitulation vs governmental demonstration) while converging on the substitution simpliciter. The companion question doc-penal-substitution targets the specific Reformed penal-mechanism framing and is K2 explicitly excluded as theory-specific; doc-vicarious-atonement is the broader Christ-died-for-us framework already addressed. Public-record carriers of denial: classical Abelardian moral-exemplar reductionism, modern liberal-mainline writers who reduce the cross to martyrdom or moral example only, the non-violent atonement school (J. Denny Weaver, Stuart Murray, some contemporary Anabaptist writers, certain feminist and womanist proposals like Brown and Parker), Gustaf Aulen's 1931 reading that dismissed substitution as Western legal-juridical accretion (though most Orthodox theologians do not follow Aulen that far), Socinians historically, and Unitarian Universalists generally. Jehovah's Witnesses affirm a ransom but reframe it in non-trinitarian terms which collapses the divine-personal substitute. Iglesia ni Cristo's denial of the deity of the one who atones similarly collapses the substitution. To deny that Christ died in the place of sinners in any substitutionary sense severs the explicit hyper / anti / paga lexical pattern of Scripture and empties 1 Cor 15:3 of its meaning. Brethren tradition holds this firmly and rightly, but so does the entire historic church; this is not a Brethren distinctive.

stem_audit.neutralized_form: Christ's death has substitutionary force; he died on behalf of and in the place of sinners, such that those for whom he died are reconciled to God through that death.
stem_audit.notes: The original stem asserts the verdict in its main clause ('Christ died for sinners in their place') and uses the Reformed-confessional framing 'a substitute death, by which those for whom He died are reconciled to God' (echoing WCF 8.5 / Heidelberg Q37-40 which are listed as confessional anchors). The stem also contains an em dash, which is banned in output. The neutralized form preserves the substitutionary lexical core (anti, hyper, paga, lytron, hilasmos) without confessional embellishment and without prejudging extent (particular vs general redemption is a distinct in-house debate).

lay_summary.reasoning: Yes, the Bible plainly teaches that Jesus died in the place of sinners, taking what they deserved. The strongest reason is the weight of small Greek words across the New Testament. Mark 10:45 says the Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom 'in place of' many. Matthew 20:28 repeats this on Jesus's own lips. Romans 5:8 says 'while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.' 2 Corinthians 5:21 says God 'made him who knew no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.' Galatians 3:13 says Christ became 'a curse for us.' 1 Peter 2:24 says he 'bore our sins in his body on the tree.' 1 Peter 3:18 says 'the righteous for the unrighteous, that he might bring us to God.' This pattern saturates the New Testament. The Old Testament foundation is Isaiah 53, where the servant is pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities, and the Lord 'has laid on him the iniquity of us all.' The Day of Atonement scapegoat in Leviticus 16 carries Israel's sins away on its own head. The strongest counterargument comes from texts that emphasize Christ as moral example or victor over evil rather than substitute. 1 Peter 2:21 calls Christ an example for us to follow. Colossians 2:15 says he disarmed the powers, triumphing over them. Some readers argue these mean the cross is mainly example or mainly victory, not substitution. But these texts do not deny substitution. They sit alongside it. The same 1 Peter 2 that calls Christ an example says three verses later that he bore our sins. The cross is example AND victory AND substitution at once. The verdict holds because the substitution language is not isolated to one passage; it runs from Isaiah through the Gospels through Paul through Peter through Hebrews through John's letters as the basic shape of the gospel.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Every historic Christian lineage affirms that Christ died in the place of sinners in some substitutionary sense. The patristic writers, especially Athanasius in 'On the Incarnation', describe Christ surrendering his body 'instead of all' and offering it to the Father; Gregory of Nyssa and the early Greek fathers also emphasize a ransom paid on our behalf. Anselm's 'Cur Deus Homo' formalized the satisfaction view in the medieval West, arguing that Christ's death pays the debt humanity owed to God's honor. The Catholic Catechism (599-618) teaches that Christ delivered himself up for our sins and that his redemptive death is the heart of God's plan of salvation. Lutheran teaching (Augsburg Confession Article III) says Christ was 'a sacrifice, not only for original guilt, but also for all actual sins of men.' Anglican teaching (39 Articles, Article 2) says Christ suffered 'to reconcile his Father to us, and to be a sacrifice for all actual sins of men.' Methodist Articles of Religion repeat the same language. Reformed and Baptist confessions teach penal substitution as the dominant mechanism. Pentecostal and Assemblies of God statements affirm Christ's vicarious atoning death as the only ground of justification. Eastern Orthodoxy emphasizes that Christ took the curse of death and triumphed over it in our place, rejecting strict penal-satisfaction framing but strongly affirming the substitution itself. Anabaptist confessions (Dordrecht Article 4) affirm Christ's atoning sacrifice for the sins of the whole human race. The lineages disagree about the mechanism, not the simpliciter substitution. Public-record denials sit outside historic Christianity. Socinians (16th and 17th century) rejected substitution as unjust. Modern liberal-mainline theology in its reductive form treats the cross as moral example only, following Abelard. The non-violent atonement school (J. Denny Weaver, some contemporary writers) rejects substitution as inherently violent and unjust. Gustaf Aulen's 1931 'Christus Victor' dismissed substitution as a Western legal accretion, though most Orthodox theologians do not follow him that far. Unitarian Universalists generally reject substitution. Jehovah's Witnesses affirm a ransom but reframe it in non-trinitarian terms which guts the divine substitute. Iglesia ni Cristo denies the deity of the one who atones, which collapses the substitution. Mormons affirm an atonement but locate it primarily in Gethsemane within a different ontology. Outright denial that Christ died in the place of sinners severs the explicit pattern of Scripture and removes the substance of 1 Corinthians 15:3.

scripture.key_term: H5375 nasa + H5445 sabal + H6293 paga hiphil + H5771 avon
scripture.force: He has borne (nasa) our griefs and carried (sabal) our sorrows; YHWH caused to meet upon him (hiphil of paga) the iniquity (avon) of us all. The causative hiphil of paga is YHWH actively transferring iniquity onto the servant. Direct lexical substitution.

scripture.key_term: H817 asham + H6293 paga + H5375 nasa
scripture.force: His soul makes an asham (guilt offering); he bore the sin of many and made intercession (paga) for the transgressors. Sacrificial-substitution language explicit.

scripture.key_term: H5564 samakh + H5771 avon + H5375 nasa
scripture.force: Aaron lays both hands on the scapegoat, confesses over it all the iniquities of Israel, and the goat bears (nasa) all their iniquities upon itself to a remote land. Foundational substitution typology behind New Testament atonement language.

The Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom in-place-of (anti) many. Anti carries strict substitutionary force; lytron is the redemption price paid to release another. The most explicit substitution saying of Jesus.
Parallel saying to Mark 10:45; doubles the canonical weight of the substitution declaration on Jesus's own lips.
The good shepherd lays down his life for (hyper) the sheep. Voluntary substitution on Jesus's own lips.
Caiaphas prophesies that one man should die for (hyper) the people; John reads this as substitutionary atonement embracing the whole nation and the scattered children of God.
Whom God set forth as a hilasterion (mercy seat / propitiation) in his blood. Day-of-Atonement typology applied to Christ; sacrificial substitution.

Christ died for (hyper) the ungodly; while we were yet sinners Christ died for us. Hyper here carries representative and substitutionary force; the explicit contrast with our state makes the in-our-place reading necessary.
Christ died for (hyper) our sins according to the Scriptures. Paul's received gospel summary; substitutionary force foundational to the kerygma.
One died for (hyper) all, therefore all died. The logic only works if his death counts as theirs, that is, substitutionary representation.
He made him who knew no sin to be sin for us (hyper hemon), so that in him we might become the righteousness of God. Direct exchange formula: he gets our status, we get his.

scripture.key_term: G5228 hyper (variant G4012 peri) + didomi heauton
scripture.force: Who gave himself for (hyper / peri) our sins, to deliver us out of the present evil age. Self-giving substitution language.

scripture.key_term: G2671 katara + G5228 hyper + G1805 exagorazo
scripture.force: Christ redeemed us out of the curse of the law, having become a curse for us (hyper hemon). The curse he absorbs is the one owed by us under Deut 21:23.

scripture.key_term: G5228 hyper + G4376 prosphora + G2378 thysia
scripture.force: Christ loved us and gave himself up for (hyper) us, an offering (prosphora) and sacrifice (thysia) to God. Sacrificial-substitution.
Who gave himself as antilytron (substitutionary ransom) for (hyper) all. Antilytron combines anti and lytron, doubling the in-place-of force.
Christ was offered once to bear (anaphero) the sins of many. Echoes Isa 53:12 directly; sacrificial vocabulary of carrying offerings up to the altar applied to Christ.

By that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all; by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified. Sacrificial substitution tied to a single definitive offering.
He himself bore (anaphero) our sins in his body on the tree. Anaphero is the sacrificial verb of bringing offerings up to the altar; Peter applies it to Christ carrying our sins.
Christ suffered once for sins, the righteous for (hyper) the unrighteous, to bring us to God. Maximally explicit substitution: the righteous one in place of the unrighteous ones.
He is the propitiation (hilasmos) for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world. Sacrificial-atonement language carrying substitutionary force.
Herein is love, not that we loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation (hilasmos) for our sins. Love and substitutionary propitiation are joined, not opposed.

Christ suffered for you, leaving you an example to follow in his steps. Some take this as the example-only counter; but the very next verses (2:22-24) name him as the sin-bearer. Example AND substitute.

Having disarmed the rulers and authorities, he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them in him. Christus-Victor strand; complicates a substitution-only frame but does not deny substitution (Col 2:14 names the legal certificate cancelled at the cross).

Through death he destroyed the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery. Christus-Victor strand; sits alongside substitution, does not exclude it.

competing_lens_verdicts.note: Substitution is the heart of covenantal atonement, with Christ as the second Adam absorbing the covenantal curse in our place.
Substitution is foundational to dispensational soteriology and present in every dispensation typologically through the sacrificial system fulfilled in Christ.
Christ's substitutionary death inaugurates the new covenant predicted in Jer 31 and ratifies it in his blood.
Substitution climaxes the Levitical typology in the antitype Christ; the day-of-atonement pattern is fulfilled in the once-for-all offering of Heb 9-10.
Affirms substitution simpliciter (Christ dies on our behalf and in our place) while emphasizing the dominant mechanism as triumph over death, devil, and sin rather than penal payment.

hermeneutics.notes: All tracked frameworks converge on substitution simpliciter. They diverge on the dominant mechanism (penal-satisfaction vs Christus-Victor vs governmental vs recapitulation). The simpliciter verdict does not require choosing among mechanisms; it requires only the lexical reading of hyper, anti, paga, anaphero, hilasmos, hilasterion, lytron, and antilytron. The mechanism question is in-house and handled separately under doc-penal-substitution (theory-specific, K2 explicitly excluded).

counter_witness.key_phrase: He surrendered His body to death instead of all, and offered it to the Father. By the sacrifice of His own body He put an end to the law of death.

counter_witness.anchor: Gregory of Nyssa, Great Catechism ch. 22-24 (CCEL NPNF Series 2 Vol 5)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ paid a ransom on behalf of humanity, taking the place of those held in bondage to death; substitution simpliciter with Christus-Victor mechanism.
Christ took our punishment without our guilt, that he might deliver us from our guilt by his punishment; substitution in our place explicit.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church 599-618, 'Christ's Redemptive Death in God's Plan of Salvation'
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures. By giving his life as a ransom for many, Jesus offered himself in our place; his death is the heart of the redemptive plan.

counter_witness.anchor: Anselm, Cur Deus Homo Bk II ch. 6-7
counter_witness.key_phrase: It is necessary that the God-man pay what is owed by the sinful human race; he alone could make satisfaction sufficient for the debt of sin.

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession Article III, Of the Son of God
counter_witness.key_phrase: That He might reconcile the Father unto us, and be a sacrifice, not only for original guilt, but also for all actual sins of men.

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles, Article 2, Of the Word or Son of God which was made very Man
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ truly suffered, was crucified, dead, and buried, to reconcile his Father to us, and to be a sacrifice, not only for original guilt, but also for all actual sins of men.

The Lord Jesus, by his perfect obedience and sacrifice of himself, hath fully satisfied the justice of God, procuring reconciliation and an everlasting inheritance for those whom the Father hath given him.

counter_witness.anchor: UMC Articles of Religion, Article II and Article XX
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ truly suffered, was crucified, dead, and buried, to reconcile his Father to us, and to be a sacrifice. The offering of Christ is that perfect redemption, propitiation, and satisfaction for all the sins of the whole world.
Christ has, through his precious blood and death on the cross, atoned for the sins of the whole human race, accomplishing the redemption of fallen humanity.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, Statement of Fundamental Truths, Art. 3 and Art. 5
counter_witness.key_phrase: Man's only hope of redemption is through the shed blood of Jesus Christ the Son of God; his vicarious and atoning death is the only sufficient ground of justification.

counter_witness.anchor: OCA, The Orthodox Faith, Volume 1, Doctrine: The Crucifixion
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ died for us. Orthodox tradition rejects strict penal-satisfaction framing but affirms that the Son of God voluntarily took our place, suffered for our salvation, and conquered death by death.

web.quote: G5228 hyper: for, on behalf of. One who does a thing for another is conceived as standing or bending over the one whom he would shield or defend. Substitutionary force.
G473 anti: opposite, instead of, in place of. Mark 10:45 his life a ransom for many: anti anchors substitutionary atonement, divine exchange.
G3083 lytron: ransom, redemption price; the price paid to free a slave. Two occurrences, both on Jesus's lips: Matt 20:28 and Mark 10:45.
G487 antilytron: a substitutionary ransom. Compound of anti (in place of) and lytron (ransom price). 1 Tim 2:6 the only NT occurrence.
G2434 hilasmos: propitiation, expiation, that which appeases. 1 John 2:2 and 4:10 use it for Christ; sacrificial-atonement vocabulary.
lytron anti pollon: ransom in place of many. Anti carries substitutionary exchange; the Son of Man gives his life as the redemption price.
hiphil of paga H6293: YHWH caused the iniquity of us all to fall upon him. Causative voice; divine action of transferring guilt to the servant.
ton me gnonta hamartian hyper hemon hamartian epoiesen: the one who knew no sin he made to be sin for us. Direct substitution formula.
genomenos hyper hemon katara: having become for us a curse. Christ takes the curse owed by us under the law's penalty.
dikaios hyper adikon: the righteous for the unrighteous. Maximally explicit substitutionary contrast in a single phrase.
Augsburg Article III: that He might reconcile the Father unto us, and be a sacrifice, not only for original guilt, but also for all actual sins of men.
UMC Article XX: The offering of Christ, once made, is that perfect redemption, propitiation, and satisfaction for all the sins of the whole world.
Athanasius: He surrendered His body to death instead of all, and offered it to the Father. The sacrifice of His own body put an end to the law of death.
Orthodox doctrine affirms Christ took our place: he assumed our death, suffered for our salvation, and conquered death by death; substitution is real though framed by Christus-Victor.
